title: Listening - Part 3: The Scents of Commerce
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a sensory psychologist, Dr. Abbi, and a retail consultant, Elliot, about how smells influence our shopping habits. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview discussing the direct link between the olfactory bulb and the limbic system, 'signature scents' for hotels, and ethical boundaries.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We are used to visual ads, but what about the invisible ones? Scent marketing is a multi-billion dollar industry. Abbi, why is smell such a powerful tool for businesses?

Abbi: [15] Unlike sight or sound, smell is the only sense that bypasses the rational brain and goes straight to the limbic system—the emotional center. [/15] It’s a direct line to memory. A certain scent can trigger a childhood memory in milliseconds, long before you've even identified what the smell is. This makes it an incredibly potent way to create an emotional bond with a brand.

Interviewer: Elliot, how do shops actually use this? Is it just about making the place smell 'nice'?

Elliot: [16] Not at all. It’s highly strategic. For example, high-end hotels now develop 'signature scents'—unique blends that are pumped through the air conditioning. [/16] When you smell that specific combination of white tea and fig in a hotel in Tokyo, and then again in London, your brain instantly tells you that you are 'home' and safe. It builds loyalty through the subconscious.

Interviewer: I’ve heard that supermarkets use the smell of fresh bread to make us buy more. Does that actually work?

Abbi: [17] It does, but not just because you want bread. It’s about 'priming.' The smell of fresh bread signals 'freshness' and 'wholesomeness' for the entire store. [/17] It puts you in a relaxed, domestic mood, which makes you less likely to count your pennies and more likely to fill your trolley. It’s about creating an atmosphere where you feel comfortable spending money.

Interviewer: Elliot, can a scent ever backfire?

Elliot: [18] Definitely. There’s a risk of 'sensory overload.' If a clothing store smells too strongly of cologne, it can actually drive customers away—especially those with sensitivities. [/18] Also, scent must be 'congruent.' If you are in a hardware store and it smells like flowers, your brain gets confused. There has to be a logical link between the product and the aroma.

Interviewer: Abbi, is this a form of 'subliminal manipulation'? Is it ethical?

Abbi: [19] It’s a gray area. We can't 'turn off' our noses. We can close our eyes or block our ears, but we have to breathe. [/19] However, most experts argue that a smell alone won't make you buy something you don't want. It just influences the *time* you spend in a store and your overall mood.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Elliot? Is this the future of branding?

Elliot: [20] We are moving toward 'multi-sensory' branding. In a digital world, the physical store has to offer something the internet can't—and that’s a full-body experience. Scent is the final frontier of that. [/20]

Interviewer: Abbi, Elliot, thank you.

questions.question: Why does Abbi consider smell to be unique among the senses?
options: A) It is the only sense that we can control with our minds.
B) It bypasses the rational part of the brain and affects emotions directly.
C) It takes much longer for the brain to process than sight.
D) It is the only sense that doesn't change as we get older.
questions.explanation: She states that smell goes straight to the limbic system, the emotional center, bypassing the rational brain.

questions.question: According to Elliot, why do hotels use 'signature scents'?
options: A) To hide the smell of cleaning chemicals from the guests.
B) To create a sense of familiarity and loyalty across different locations.
C) To encourage guests to buy the perfume used in the lobby.
D) To make the air in the hotel easier to breathe for travelers.
questions.explanation: He explains that smelling the same scent in different cities makes guests feel 'at home'.

questions.question: What is the psychological effect of the smell of fresh bread in a shop?
options: A) It makes customers believe that they are very hungry.
B) It creates a general feeling of freshness and relaxation.
C) It forces people to spend money only on bakery items.
D) It reminds people that they need to go home and cook.
questions.explanation: Abbi mentions it signals 'freshness' and puts people in a 'relaxed, domestic mood'.

questions.question: Elliot uses the example of a hardware store to show that
options: A) flowers are always a good way to improve a shop's atmosphere.
B) scent marketing is only effective in luxury clothing stores.
C) there must be a logical connection between a smell and a product.
D) hardware stores are usually too large for scent marketing to work.
questions.explanation: He says the scent must be 'congruent' and that a hardware store smelling of flowers would confuse the brain.

questions.question: Abbi suggests that scent marketing is ethically complicated because
options: A) the chemicals used in the scents are often very expensive.
B) human beings cannot choose to stop smelling their environment.
C) it can make people buy things that are dangerous for their health.
D) most people do not have a very good sense of smell.
questions.explanation: She notes that 'we can't turn off our noses' and 'we have to breathe', unlike closing our eyes.

questions.question: Elliot believes that physical stores must use scent because
options: A) it is the only way to compete with the prices of online shops.
B) it provides a multi-sensory experience that the internet cannot match.
C) digital devices will eventually be able to produce smells.
D) customers are no longer interested in looking at visual advertisements.
questions.explanation: He says physical stores must offer a 'full-body experience' that isn't available online.
